did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep CNN transformer hybrid model (combining deep convolutional neural networks and vision transformers) with focal loss to classify skin lesions in dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel end-to-end CNN transformer hybrid model with focal loss for skin lesion classification. It details original experimental methods, including dataset splitting (ISIC 2018), implementation details (PyTorch, hyperparameters), and presents new quantitative results (accuracy, AUC) comparing the proposed model against baselines, which characterizes it as an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: A Deep CNN Transformer Hybrid Model for Skin Lesion Classification of Dermoscopic Images Using Focal Loss
brief_summary: This study proposes an end-to-end deep CNN transformer hybrid model with focal loss to classify skin lesions in dermoscopic images, addressing the challenge of extreme class imbalance. The model utilizes a CNN to extract local features and a vision transformer to model global semantic information before classification via a multi-layer perceptron head. The authors evaluated their approach on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 dataset, which contains 10,015 labeled images across seven classes. Experimental results demonstrated that the hybrid model combined with focal loss achieved an overall accuracy of 89.48% and a mean AUC of 0.96, significantly outperforming baseline models and existing methods. The findings validate that this architecture effectively mitigates class imbalance issues in skin cancer classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion diagnostic categories (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis lesion, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into one of seven classes using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories rather than continuous values. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, AUC, precision, recall, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public large-scale dataset of dermoscopic images published by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for Task 3 (lesion classification). It contains labeled dermoscopic images with extreme class imbalance.

per_class.NV: highest number of samples (approx. 58 times more than DF)

use.training: Used for training the deep CNN transformer hybrid model with focal loss.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed models (accuracy, AUC, etc.).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the uncertainty of the overall accuracy (OA) for the proposed models (e.g., 89.48 ± 1.34).
To display the number of correctly classified samples for each class and details for each misclassified class, aiding in the evaluation of performance across imbalanced classes.
To calculate the arithmetic mean of statistical indicators (Precision, Recall, F1, AUC) for all categories, providing a class-independent performance measure.
To compute a global average by counting the sums of true positives, false negatives, and false positives across all classes.
To calculate Precision and Recall by multiplying the values of each category by the proportion of the category in the total sample, accounting for class imbalance.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To intuitively evaluate the quality of the classifier by plotting the True Positive rate against the False Positive rate.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported performance metrics with 95% confidence intervals and described improvements qualitatively (e.g., 'significantly high performance', 'significant improvement') without statistical hypothesis testing.

ISIC 2018.notes: The Hybrid CNN Transformer model with Focal Loss (CNNViTfc) achieved the best performance. The OA improved from 74.21% (baseline ResNet-50 with CE) to 89.48%. The mean AUC reached 0.96 (micro) and 0.90 (macro), representing improvements of 7% and 18% respectively over baselines. ViT models performed poorly on MEL classification compared to other classes, but the hybrid model with FL mitigated this. The NV class correct classification improved from 1020 to 1239, and BKL from 133 to 168. MEL correct classification decreased from 175 to 155 in some hybrid variants compared to CE, but overall performance was superior.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 (used for CNN feature extraction, implying ImageNet pretraining context though not explicitly stated as loaded weights in the text, standard for ResNet-50)

architecture_details.backbone: Hybrid of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Vision Transformer (ViT)
architecture_details.cnn_component: First ten layers of ResNet-50 used as feature extractor. Consists of a convolutional layer (7x7 kernel), max pooling layer, and residual blocks.
architecture_details.transformer_component: Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder. Includes patch embedding layer (kernel size and stride of 1 for channel adjustment), class token, and position embeddings (trainable parameters). Transformer encoder consists of L repeated encoder blocks. Each block contains Layer Normalization (LN), Multi-Head Attention (MHA), and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) block with skip connections.
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Multi-Head Attention (MHA). Scaled dot-product attention: Attention(V, K, Q) = Softmax((QK^T)/sqrt(d_k))V. Q, K, V are linear projections of input R. h heads are concatenated.
architecture_details.mlp_block: Two fully connected (FC) layers stacked with Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) activation and dropout layer.
architecture_details.normalization: Layer Normalization (LN) applied before MHA and MLP blocks.
loss_functions_tested: Focal Loss (FL) with alpha=0.25 and gamma=2

training_details.training_data_split: ISIC 2018 dataset split into Training (0.7), Validation (0.1), Testing (0.2)

classification_or_output_head.details: The output of the ViT (global attention encoding) is fed into an MLP head for classification into 7 classes.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNNViTce (Hybrid CNN Transformer with Cross Entropy)
CNNViTwce (Hybrid CNN Transformer with Weighted Cross Entropy)

conclusions: The proposed end-to-end deep CNN transformer hybrid model combined with focal loss (FL) significantly outperforms existing baseline models and state-of-the-art methods in classifying skin lesions from dermoscopic images.

The hybrid architecture effectively addresses the extreme class imbalance present in the ISIC 2018 dataset, particularly improving performance on underrepresented classes compared to models using standard cross-entropy loss.

The CNN transformer hybrid model with focal loss achieved an overall accuracy of 89.48% and a mean AUC of 0.96 (micro-average) and 0.90 (macro-average), representing improvements of 7% and 18% respectively over baselines.
The model achieved the highest AUC of 93.3% for melanoma (MEL) classification among the tested variants, validating its efficacy for critical skin cancer detection tasks.

While Vision Transformer (ViT) models alone performed poorly on MEL classification, the hybrid approach mitigated this limitation, demonstrating the benefit of combining local feature extraction (CNN) with global semantic modeling (ViT).

The study demonstrates that focusing on data imbalance via focal loss within a hybrid architecture is a meaningful and effective strategy for improving diagnostic performance without requiring complex ensemble methods or additional parameters.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which exhibits extreme class imbalance (e.g., NV class has ~58 times more samples than DF class), potentially limiting generalizability to other datasets or real-world distributions.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance improvements, relying instead on qualitative descriptions and confidence intervals.
The proposed hybrid CNN-Transformer model with Focal Loss achieved an overall accuracy of 89.48% and mean AUC of 0.96, significantly outperforming baselines like ResNet-50 (74.21% accuracy).

While the model improved performance on underrepresented classes like BKL and NV, it showed a decrease in correctly classified Melanoma (MEL) cases compared to some baselines, though it still achieved the highest MEL AUC (93.3%) among tested variants.
The research was funded with no external funding, and the authors declare no conflicts of interest.
The study acknowledges that ViT models alone performed poorly on MEL classification, highlighting the necessity of the hybrid CNN-ViT architecture for effective feature extraction in this domain.

The dataset split used was 70% training, 10% validation, and 20% testing, which is a standard but fixed split; external validation on other datasets (e.g., ISIC 2019/2020) is suggested as future work.
